elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that the minimal tensor product of a $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ satisfying Kadison's similarity property and a nuclear $C^*$-algebra $\cl B,$ satisfies Kadison's similarity property and its length $\ell\left(\cl A \tens \limits_{min \cl B\right)$ is less than $3\,\ell(\cl A).$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Given a $C^*$-algebra $ \mathcal A$ and a bounded homomorphism $\rho\colon \mathcal A\rightarrow \mathcal B(H),$ where $H$ is a Hilbert space, does there exist an invertible operator $S\in \mathcal B(H)$ such that $\pi (\cdot)=S^{-1}\,\rho(\cdot)\,S$ defines a $*$-homomorphism of $\cl A$? If this happens, we say that $\rho$ is similar to $\pi.$ We say that $\mathcal A$ satisfies the similarity property ((SP)) if every bounded homomorphism $\rho\colon \mathcal A\rightarrow \mathcal B(H),$ where $H$ is a Hilbert space, is similar to a $*$-homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-003: The above property was introduced by Kadison in \cite{kad where he conjectured that all $C^*$-algebras satisfy the similarity property.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Kadison's similarity problem is equivalent to a number of questions, including the problem of hyperreflexivity of all von Neumann algebras, the derivation problem, the invariant operator range problem and the problem of finite length of $C^*$-algebras \cite{pisier1 .}
elements.sent-2411-005: The author and Eleftherakis in their joint work \cite{ele-pap , proved that Kadison's similarity problem is equivalent to the following property, which they named {\bf (CHH): {\em Every hyperreflexive separably acting von Neumann algebra is completely hyperreflexive .}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Recently, in \cite{pap the author proved that Kadison's similarity problem is also equivalent to the following property {\bf (EP): {\em Every separably acting von Neumann algebra with a cyclic vector is hyperreflexive .}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: We say that a von Neumann algebra $\cl M$ satisfies the weak similarity property ((WSP)) if every $\rm{w ^*$-continuous, unital and bounded homomorphism $u\colon \cl M\rightarrow \cl B(H),$ where $H$ is a Hilbert space, is similar to a $*$-homomorphism.}
elements.sent-2411-008: The connection between (SP) and (WSP) is given by the following lemma.
elements.sent-2411-009: The algebra $\cl A$ satisfies (SP) if and only if its second dual $\cl A^{**}$ satisfies (WSP).
elements.sent-2411-010: If $\cl A$ is a nuclear $C^*$-algebra then $\ell(\cl A)\leq 2$ \cite{pn .}
elements.sent-2411-011: If $\cl A$ is a $C^*$-algebra with no tracial states, then $\ell(\cl A)\leq 3$ \cite{pis .}
elements.sent-2411-012: If $\cl M$ is a type $II_1$ factor with property $\Gamma,$ then $\ell(\cl M)=3$ \cite{chris1 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: For any $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ it holds that $\ell\left(\cl A \tens \limits_{min \cl K\right)\leq 3,$ where $\cl K$ is the $C^*$-algebra of compact operators on $\ell^2(\bb N)$ \cite{haag, pis .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: It is not known whether there exists a unital $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ with $\ell(\cl A)>3.$ An affirmative answer to Kadison's similarity problem would imply the existence of a positive integer $\ell_0$ such that $\ell(\cl A)\leq \ell_0$ for every $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ \cite[Proposition 15]{pis .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In Section 2, we prove that the minimal tensor product of a unital $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ satisfying (SP) with a unital scattered $C^*$-algebra $\cl B$, satisfies (SP), using essentially biduals of $C^*$-tensor products and Theorem 4.5 in \cite{ele-pap .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In Section 3, we prove that the minimal tensor product of a $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ satisfying (SP) and a nuclear $C^*$-algebra $\cl B,$ satisfies (SP) and its length $\ell\left(\cl A \tens \limits_{min \cl B\right)$ is less than $3\,\ell(\cl A).$ Furthermore, we show that a $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ satisfies (SP) if and only if $M_n(\cl A)\cong \cl A\tens \limits_{min M_n(\bb C)$ satisfies (SP).}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In what follows, $\cl B(H)$ is the $C^*$-algebra of all linear and bounded operators from the Hilbert space $H$ to itself.
elements.sent-2411-018: For two $C^*$-algebras $\cl A$ and $\cl B,$ $\,\cl A\tens \limits_{min \cl B$ denotes their minimal (spatial) tensor product.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, if $\cl M$ and $\cl N$ are von Neumann algebras, then we denote by $\cl M\,\bar \otimes\,\cl N$ their normal spatial tensor product.
elements.sent-2411-021: We remind the reader the notions of a completely bounded map, of a nuclear $C^*$-algebra and an injective von Neumann algebra that will be used in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-022: For each $n\in\mathbb N$ consider the matrix amplification \begin{equation}u_n\colon M_n(\mathcal X)\to M_n(\mathcal Y),\,\, u_n((X_{i,j}))=(u(X_{i,j})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-023: If $\,\sup_{n ||u_n||<\infty, $ then we say that $u$ is completely bounded with norm \begin{equation}||u||_{cb}:=\sup_{n} ||u_n||.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Effros and Lance in their joint work \cite{ef-lan proved that a $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ is nuclear if and only if $\cl A^{**}$ is injective.}
elements.sent-2411-025: For further details on nuclear $C^*$-algebras see \cite{bo, ef-choi, ef-lan, la .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In \cite{ele-pap the author and Eleftherakis proved that the normal spatial tensor product of an injective von Neumann algebra and a von Neumann algebra satisfying (WSP), satisfies (WSP).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Therefore, it is natural to ask whether the minimal tensor product of a nuclear $C^*$-algebra with a $C^*$-algebra satisfying (SP), satisfies (SP).
elements.sent-2411-028: In this section we deal with the case of a unital $C^*$-algebra satisfying (SP) and a unital scattered $C^*$-algebra.
elements.sent-2411-029: Using essentially biduals of $C^*$-tensor products and Theorem 4.5 in \cite{ele-pap , we prove that the minimal tensor product of a unital $C^*$-algebra satisfying (SP) with a unital scattered $C^*$-algebra, satisfies (SP).}
elements.sent-2411-030: As we will see below, a scattered $C^*$-algebra is a nuclear $C^*$-algebra.
elements.sent-2411-031: We start with the definition of a scattered $C^*$-algebra.
elements.sent-2411-032: Since $\cl B^{**}$ is a direct sum of type $I$ factors and every type $I$ factor is injective, we deduce that $\cl B^{**}$ is injective.
elements.sent-2411-034: The finite dimensional $C^*$-algebras $M_n(\bb C),\,\,n\in\bb N,$ as well as the $C^*$-algebra $\,\cl K$ of the compact operators on $\ell^2(\bb N).$.
elements.sent-2411-035: A topological space $X$ is called scattered if every nonempty subset $U$ of $X$ contains an isolated point in $U.$ The $C^*$-algebra $C(X),$ where $X$ is a scattered compact topological is a scattered $C^*$-algebra \cite{rudin .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Compact $C^*$-algebras $\cl A$ (i.e. $\cl A$ is an ideal of $\cl A^{**}$) \cite{kap, yl .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Huruya in \cite{hur proved that if $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ are $C^*$-algebras and $\cl A$ or $\cl B$ is scattered, then $\left(\cl A\tens \limits_{min \cl B\right)^{**}\cong \cl A^{**}\,\bar\otimes\,\cl B^{** .$ In 1987, Quigg in his article \cite{quigg proved that if $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ are $C^*$-algebras, then $\left(\cl A\tens \limits_{min \cl B\right)^{**}$ and $\cl A^{**}\,\bar\otimes\,\cl B^{**}$ are isomorphic if and only if $\cl A$ or $\cl B$ is scattered.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: According to Lemma \ref{SP-WSP it suffices to prove that $\left(\cl A\tens \limits_{min \cl B\right)^{**}$ satisfies (WSP).}}

elements.sent-2411-041: The von Neumann algebra $\cl B^{**}$ is injective and according to Lemma \ref{SP-WSP the von Neumann algebra $\cl A^{**}$ satisfies (WSP).}
elements.sent-2411-042: By Theorem 4.5 in \cite{ele-pap yields that $\cl A^{**}\bar \otimes\, \cl B^{**}$ satisfies (WSP), that is $\left(\cl A\tens \limits_{min \cl B\right)^{**}$ satisfies (WSP), as desired.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In this section we prove that the minimal tensor product of a $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ satisfying (SP) and a nuclear $C^*$-algebra $\cl B,$ satisfies (SP) and its length $\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{min \cl B\right)$ is less than $3\,\ell(\cl A).$ We use essentially a characterisation of nuclear $C^*$-algebras given by Pisier in \cite[Theorem 1]{pairs and basic facts of the notion of the length for a pair of two unital $C^*$-algebras \cite{length, pairs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: We also use Haagerup's remarkable result that a bounded homomorphism from a $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ into $\cl B(H),$ where $H$ is a Hilbert space, is similar to a $*$-homomorphism if and only if it is completely bounded \cite[Theorem 1.10]{haag .}
elements.sent-2411-045: First we prove the above statement for the unital case.

elements.sent-2411-057: Before this we prove a useful lemma.
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $f\colon \cl A\to \cl B$ be a $*$-isomorphism between $\cl A$ and $\cl B.$ If $\ell(\cl B)=\infty$ then by \cite{pisier3 $\,\cl B$ does not satisfy (SP), and thus $\cl A$ does not satisfy (SP) which implies that $\ell(\cl A)=\ell(\cl B)=\infty.$ Assume that $\ell(\cl B)<\infty.$ Since the relation $\cong$ of isomorphism between $C^*$-algebras is symmetric, it suffices to prove that $\ell(\cl A)\leq \ell(\cl B).$ To this end, let $\pi\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H)$ be a bounded representation of $\cl A$ on the Hilbert space $H.$ The map $\tilde{\pi =\pi\circ f^{-1}\colon \cl B\to \cl B(H)$ is a bounded representation of $\cl B,$ and thus $||\tilde{\pi ||_{cb}\leq C\,||\tilde{\pi ||^{\ell(\cl B)}$ for some constant $C>0.$ Since $\pi=\tilde{\pi}\circ f$ and $f$ is completely contractive, we have \begin{equation}||\pi||_{cb}\leq ||\tilde{\pi}||_{cb}\,||f||_{cb}\leq C\,||\tilde{\pi}||^{\ell(\cl B)}\leq C\,||\pi||^{\ell(\cl B)}.\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-069: If $\ell(\cl A)\leq 2,$ then by \cite{pn $\,\cl A$ is a nuclear $C^*$-algebra, and thus $\cl A\tens\limits_{min \cl B$ is nuclear as well, so $\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{min}\cl B\right)=2<6=3\cdot 2.$ If $\ell(\cl A)=3,$ then $\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{min}\cl B\right)\leq 9.$ Maybe this case generates examples of $C^*$-algebras with length $\geq 4.$ \end{rem \begin{ex For any Hilbert space $H$ and any discrete amenable group $G$ the $C^*$-algebra $\cl B(H)\tens\limits_{min C^*(G)$ satisfies (SP).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Let $\cl A$ be a $C^*$-algebra satisfying (SP) and $n\in\bb N.$ Since $M_n(\cl A)\cong \cl A\tens\limits_{min M_n(\bb C)$ and $M_n(\bb C)$ is nuclear, Theorem \ref{gen implies that $M_n(\cl A)$ satisfies (SP).}}
elements.sent-2411-071: In the following Proposition we prove the converse direction.
elements.sent-2411-072: We assume that $M_n(\cl A)$ satisfies (SP) and let $\rho\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H),$ where $H$ is a Hilbert space, be a bounded homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-073: We consider the $n$-th amplification \begin{equation}\rho_n\colon M_n(\cl A)\to \cl B(H^n),\,\,\rho_n([A_{ij}])=[\rho(A_{ij})]\end{equation} which is a bounded homomorphism of $M_n(\cl A)$ into $\cl B(H^n).$ Since $M_n(\cl A)$ satisfies (SP), by \cite[Theorem 1.10]{haag the map $\rho_n$ is completely bounded, and thus $\rho$ is completely bounded, as desired.}
elements.sent-2411-074: We conclude this section with some results on type $II_1$ factors.

elements.sent-2411-083: Other results on similarities for tensor products of certain $C^*$-algebras as well as on similarities for tensor products of type $II_1$ factors exist in \cite{pop, pop1 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: We prove that if the unital $C^*$-algebras $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ satisfy Kadison's similarity property and the length $L=L\left(\cl A\tens \limits_{max}\cl B\right)$ of their maximal tensor product is finite, then $\,\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ satisfies Kadison's similarity property with similarity length $\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)\leq L \max\left\{\ell(\cl A),\,\ell(\cl B)\right\}.$.
elements.sent-2411-085: If $\cl A$ is a nuclear $C^*$-algebra, then $\ell(\cl A)\leq 2$ \cite{pn .}
elements.sent-2411-086: By combining Lemma \ref{SP-WSP and the fact that a unital $C^*$-algebra is nuclear if and only if its second dual is injective \cite[Theorem 6.4]{ef-lan , the following question occurs naturally: (1) {\em Does the minimal tensor product of a nuclear unital $C^*$-algebra with a unital $C^*$-algebra satisfying (SP), also satisfy (SP) ? More generally : (2) {\em Does the maximal tensor product of two unital $C^*$-algebras satisfying (SP), also satisfy (SP) ? In Section 2, we set the stage by presenting definitions and lemmas that are useful for the next section.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, we prove that if there exist unital $C^*$-algebras $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ such that $d_1(\cl A,\,\cl B)<\infty$ whereas $d(\cl A,\,\cl B)=\infty,$ as these quantities defined by Pisier in \cite{pairs , then the $C^*$-algebra $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ does not satisfy (SP).}
elements.sent-2411-088: In Section 3 we prove the main theorem of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-089: Namely, in Theorem \ref{main we prove that if the unital $C^*$-algebras $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ satisfy (SP) and the length $L$ of their maximal tensor product is finite, then $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ satisfies (SP) and its similarity length $\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)$ is less than $L \max\left\{\ell(\cl A),\,\ell(\cl B)\right\}.$ This fact answers partially question (2).}
elements.sent-2411-090: In \cite{pairs , Pisier proved that if $\cl A$ is a unital and nuclear $C^*$-algebra and $\cl B$ is an arbitrary unital $C^*$-algebra, then $L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)\leq 3,$ and so question (1) above has an affirmative answer in the unital case.}
elements.sent-2411-091: We generalise this result to the non-unital case as well.
elements.sent-2411-092: For two $C^*$-algebras $\cl A$ and $\cl B,$ $\,\cl A\tens \limits_{min \cl B$ denotes their minimal (spatial) tensor product whereas $\cl A\tens\limits_{max \cl B$ denotes their maximal tensor product.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: We remind the reader the notions of a completely bounded map and a nuclear $C^*$-algebra that will be used in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-094: If $\,\sup_{n ||u_n||<\infty, $ then we say that $u$ is completely bounded with cb norm \begin{equation}||u||_{cb}:=\sup_{n} ||u_n||.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-095: We begin this section with the definition of the length for a pair of $C^*$-subalgebras \cite{pairs .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Let $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ be $C^*$-subalgebras of a $C^*$-algebra $\cl Z.$ For every $n\in\bb N$ we will view $M_n(\cl A)$ and $M_n(\cl B)$ as $C^*$-subalgebras of $M_n(\cl Z),$ so that if $x_1\in M_n(\cl A)$ and $x_2\in M_n(\cl B),$ then the product $x_1 x_2$ belongs to $M_n(\cl Z),$ and similarly for a product of rectangular matrices.
elements.sent-2411-098: We say that $L(\cl Z;\,\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq d$ if there is a constant $C$ such that for any $n\in\bb N$ and any $x\in M_n(\cl Z)$ with $||x||_{M_n(\cl Z) <1$ and for any $\epsilon>0,$ there exists $N\in\bb N$ for which we can find matrices $x_1,\,x_2,\,...,x_d$ and $y_1,\,y_2,\,...,y_d,$ with entries either all in $\cl A$ or all in $\cl B,$ where $x_1,\,x_2,\,...,x_d$ are of sizes respectively, $n\times N,\,N\times N,\,...,N\times N$ and $N\times n,$ and similarly for $y_1,\,y_2,\,...,y_d,$ satisfying \begin{equation}\prod_{j=1}^{d}||x_j||+\prod_{j=1}^{d}||y_j||<C\end{equation} and finally \begin{equation}\nor{x-\prod_{j=1}^{d}x_j-\prod_{j=1}^{d}y_j}<\epsilon.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-099: In what follows, let $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ be unital $C^*$-algebras.
elements.sent-2411-100: We identify $\cl A$ with $\cl A\,\otimes 1_{\cl B}$ and $\cl B$ with $1_{\cl A}\,\otimes \cl B$ and we view them as unital $C^*$-subalgebras of the unital $C^*$-algebra $\cl Z=\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B.$ \begin{defn}\cite{pairs We define the length of $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ to be \begin{equation}L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right):=L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B;\, \cl A\,\otimes 1_{\cl B},\,1_{\cl A}\,\otimes \cl B\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right):=L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B;\, \cl A\,\otimes 1_{\cl B},\,1_{\cl A}\,\otimes \cl B\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, the following definitions are meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-103: Obviously, $d_1(\cl A,\,\cl B)=\infty$ implies $d(\cl A,\,\cl B)=\infty.$ Assume that $d(\cl A,\,\cl B)=d<\infty.$ There exists $M>0$ such that for any pair $u\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H),\,v\colon\cl B\to \cl B(H)$ of completely bounded unital homomorphisms with commuting ranges, the homomorphism $u v$ is completely bounded on $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ with \begin{equation}||u v||_{cb}\leq M\,\max\left\{||u||_{cb},||v||_{cb}\right\}^d,\end{equation} which implies that for the same constant $M>0$ and for any pair $(u,v)$ as above, the homomorphism $u v$ is bounded with norm \begin{equation}||u v||\leq ||u v||_{cb}\leq M \max\left\{||u||_{cb},||v||_{cb}\right\}^d.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-104: Therefore, $d_1(\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq d=d(\cl A,\,\cl B).$.
elements.sent-2411-105: In \cite[Remark 25]{pairs , Pisier proved that under the hypothesis that for any pair $u\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H),\,v\colon \cl B\to \cl B(H)$ of completely bounded unital homomorphisms with commuting ranges, the homomorphism $u v$ is completely bounded on $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B,$ then $d(\cl A,\,\cl B)<\infty$ and, moreover, $d(\cl A,\,\cl B)=L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right).$ Assuming now that for any pair $u\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H),\,v\colon \cl B\to \cl B(H)$ of completely bounded unital homomorphisms with commuting ranges, the homomorphism $u v$ is bounded on $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B,$ then Proposition \ref{ineq and Lemma 22 in \cite{pairs imply that \begin{equation}d_1(\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq d(\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq d,\end{equation} where $d$ is the minimun integer having the property $L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)\leq d.$ However, according to \cite[Remark 25]{pairs , if we only assume that $L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)<\infty,$ we cannot verify in full generality that $d(\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right).$ \begin{prop Let $d_1=d_1(\cl A,\cl B)<\infty.$ If the $C^*$-algebra $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ satisfies (SP), then \begin{equation}L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)=d(\cl A,\cl B)\leq \ell\,d_1(\cl A,\,\cl B),\end{equation} where $\ell$ is the finite length of the $C^*$-algebra $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B.$ \end{prop.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}d_1(\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq d(\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq d,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)=d(\cl A,\cl B)\leq \ell\,d_1(\cl A,\,\cl B),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: Let $u\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H),\,v\colon \cl B\to \cl B(H)$ be completely bounded unital homomorphisms with commuting ranges.
elements.sent-2411-107: According to our hypothesis, the homomorphism $u v$ is bounded on $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ with norm \begin{equation}\label{d_1} ||u v||\leq M \max\left\{||u||_{cb},||v||_{cb}\right\}^{d_1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-108: Since $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ satisfies (SP) it follows that $u v$ is similar to a $*$-homomorphism, which implies that $u v$ is completely bounded on $\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ and from \cite{pisier3 its cb norm satisfies the inequality \begin{equation}\label{length1} ||u v||_{cb}\leq \tilde{M} ||u v||^{\ell}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-109: By combining (\ref{d_1 ) and (\ref{length1 ) we have \begin{equation}||u v||_{cb}\leq \tilde{M} M^{\ell} \max\left\{||u||_{cb},||v||_{cb}\right\}^{\ell d_1}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Therefore, $d(\cl A,\,\cl B)\leq \ell d_1<\infty$ and by Theorem 24 in \cite{pairs , we deduce that $L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)=d(\cl A,\cl B)\leq \ell d_1.$.}
elements.sent-2411-111: In this section we prove the main theorem of the paper, namely: \begin{thm Let $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ be unital $C^*$-algebras satisfying (SP) and such that $L=L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)<\infty.$ Then $\,\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B$ satisfies (SP) with similarity length $\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\right)\leq L \max\left\{\ell(\cl A),\,\ell(\cl B)\right\}.$ \end{thm.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: We may assume that $\ell(\cl A)\geq \ell(\cl B).$ Let $\pi\colon \cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\to \cl B(H)$ be a unital bounded homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-113: The maps \begin{equation}\pi_1\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H),\,\,\pi_1(a)=\pi(a\otimes 1_{\cl B})\end{equation} \begin{equation}\pi_2\colon \cl B\to \cl B(H),\,\,\pi_2(b)=\pi(1_{\cl A}\otimes b)\end{equation} are unital bounded homomorphisms of $\cl A$ and $\cl B,$ respectively, into $\cl B(H)$ with commuting ranges.
elements.sent-2411-114: Since $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ satisfy (SP), $\,\pi_1$ and $\pi_2$ are similar to $*$-homomorphisms, and thus $\pi_1$ and $\pi_2$ are completely bounded.
elements.sent-2411-115: Proposition 12 in \cite{length implies the existence of an invertible operator $S\in\cl B(H)$ such that both homomorphisms \begin{equation}x\mapsto \rho_{j}(x)=S\,\pi_{j}(x)\,S^{-1},\,\,j=1,2\end{equation} are completely contractive on $\cl A$ and $\cl B,$ respectively, with commuting ranges.}
elements.sent-2411-116: By the fact that $\cl A$ and $\cl B$ are $C^*$-algebras, $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ are $*$-homomorphisms, and thus $\rho\colon \cl A\tens\limits_{max}\cl B\to \cl B(H)$ given by $\rho(a\otimes b)=\rho_1(a)\,\rho_2(b),\,\,a\in\cl A,\,b\in\cl B$ is a $*$-homomorphism similar to $\pi$ since for all $a\in\cl A,\,b\in\cl B$ we have \begin{equation}S\,\pi(a\otimes b)\,S^{-1}=(S \pi_1(a) S^{-1})(S \pi_2(a) S^{-1})=\rho_1(a) \rho_2(b)=\rho(a\otimes b).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-117: Moreover, by \cite[Proposition 12]{length it holds that \begin{equation}\label{1st} ||\pi||_{cb}\leq K \max\left\{||\pi_1||_{cb},\,||\pi_2||_{cb}\right\}^{L}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Finally, from \cite{pisier3 we have \begin{equation}\label{similarity length} ||\pi_1||_{cb}\leq c_1(\cl A)\,||\pi_1||^{\ell(\cl A)}\leq c_1(\cl A)\, ||\pi||^{\ell(\cl A)}.\end{equation}. \begin{equation}\label{similarity length-1} ||\pi_2||_{cb}\leq c_2(\cl B)\,||\pi_2||^{\ell(\cl B)}\leq c_2(\cl B)\,||\pi||^{\ell(\cl A)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\label{similarity length} ||\pi_1||_{cb}\leq c_1(\cl A)\,||\pi_1||^{\ell(\cl A)}\leq c_1(\cl A)\, ||\pi||^{\ell(\cl A)}.\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\label{similarity length-1} ||\pi_2||_{cb}\leq c_2(\cl B)\,||\pi_2||^{\ell(\cl B)}\leq c_2(\cl B)\,||\pi||^{\ell(\cl A)}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-119: By combining (\ref{1st ), (\ref{similarity length ) and (\ref{similarity length-1 ) we obtain \begin{equation}||\pi||_{cb}\leq \tilde{K}\,||\pi||^{L\,\ell(\cl A)},\end{equation} as desired.}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Indeed, the $C^*$-algebra $\cl B(H)\tens\limits_{max}\cl B(H)$ satisfies (SP) (as a $C^*$-algebra without tracial states).
elements.sent-2411-121: However, by \cite[Corollary 29]{pairs we have $L\left(\cl B(H)\tens\limits_{max}\cl B(H)\right)=\infty.$ This example shows that compression strategy cannot work through the use of similarity degree.}
elements.sent-2411-122: If $\pi\colon \cl A\tens\limits_{min}\cl B\to \cl B(H)$ is a unital bounded homomorphism such that $\pi|_{\cl B}$ is completely bounded, then $\pi$ is completely bounded with \begin{equation}||\pi||_{cb}\leq C \max\left\{||\pi|_{\cl A}||_{cb},\,||\pi|_{\cl B}||_{cb}\right\}^3.\end{equation}. \end{prop.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The map $\pi|_{\cl A}\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H),\,\,\pi(a)=\pi(a\otimes 1_{\cl B )$ is a unital bounded homomorphism of $\cl A$ into $\cl B(H).$ The $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ satsifies (SP) as a nuclear one, and thus $\pi|_{\cl A}$ is completely bounded.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Since $L\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{min}\cl B\right)\leq 3$ \cite{pairs , it follows from Lemma 22 in \cite{pairs that $\pi$ is completely bounded with cb-norm $||\pi||_{cb}\leq C \max\left\{||\pi|_{\cl A ||_{cb ,\,||\pi|_{\cl B ||_{cb}\right\}^3.$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: The above Proposition generalises Corollary 2.3 in \cite{pop and has a lot of other corollaries as well.}
elements.sent-2411-126: We present them below: \begin{cor The tensor product of a nuclear unital $C^*$-algebra $\cl A$ with a unital $C^*$-algebra $\cl B$ satisfying (SP), also satisfies (SP).}
elements.sent-2411-127: Let $f\colon \cl A\to \cl B$ be a $*$-isomorphism between $\cl A$ and $\cl B.$ If $\ell(\cl B)=\infty$ then by \cite{pisier3 the $C^*$-algebra $\,\cl B$ does not satisfy (SP), and thus $\cl A$ does not satisfy (SP) which implies that $\ell(\cl A)=\ell(\cl B)=\infty.$ Assume that $\ell(\cl B)<\infty.$ Since the relation $\cong$ of isomorphism between $C^*$-algebras is symmetric, it suffices to prove that $\ell(\cl A)\leq \ell(\cl B).$ To this end, let $\pi\colon \cl A\to \cl B(H)$ be a bounded representation of $\cl A$ on the Hilbert space $H.$ The map $\tilde{\pi =\pi\circ f^{-1}\colon \cl B\to \cl B(H)$ is a bounded representation of $\cl B,$ and thus $||\tilde{\pi ||_{cb}\leq c(\cl B)\,||\tilde{\pi ||^{\ell(\cl B) .$ Since $\pi=\tilde{\pi}\circ f$ and $f$ is completely contractive, we have \begin{equation}||\pi||_{cb}\leq ||\tilde{\pi}||_{cb}\,||f||_{cb}\leq c(\cl B)\,||\tilde{\pi}||^{\ell(\cl B)}\leq c(\cl B)\,||\pi||^{\ell(\cl B)}.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}||\pi||_{cb}\leq ||\tilde{\pi}||_{cb}\,||f||_{cb}\leq c(\cl B)\,||\tilde{\pi}||^{\ell(\cl B)}\leq c(\cl B)\,||\pi||^{\ell(\cl B)}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-128: We are treating now the non-unital version of Corollary \ref{sp-nuc .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Then $\cl A\tens\limits_{min \cl B$ satisfies (SP) and $\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{min}\cl B\right)\leq 3 \max\left\{\ell(\cl A),\,\ell(\cl B)\right\}.$ \end{prop.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: According to Corollary \ref{sp-nuc , the $C^*$-algebra $\,\cl A^1\tens\limits_{min \cl B^1$ satisfies (SP).}}
elements.sent-2411-131: By \cite[Remark 6]{pisier4 , the fact that $L\left(\cl A^1\tens\limits_{min}\cl B^1\right)\leq 3$ \cite{pairs and Theorem \ref{main , we have \begin{equation}\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{min} \cl B\right)\leq \ell\left(\cl A^1\tens\limits_{min} \cl B^1\right)\leq 3 \max\left\{\ell(\cl A^1),\,\ell(\cl B^1)\right\}=3 \max\left\{\ell(\cl A),\,\ell(\cl B)\right\},\end{equation} and so $\cl A\tens\limits_{min \cl B$ satisfies (SP) \cite{pisier3 .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-034: \begin{equation}\ell\left(\cl A\tens\limits_{min} \cl B\right)\leq \ell\left(\cl A^1\tens\limits_{min} \cl B^1\right)\leq 3 \max\left\{\ell(\cl A^1),\,\ell(\cl B^1)\right\}=3 \max\left\{\ell(\cl A),\,\ell(\cl B)\right\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-132: Let $\cl A$ be a $C^*$-algebra satisfying (SP) and $n\in\bb N.$ Since $M_n(\cl A)\cong \cl A\tens\limits_{min M_n(\bb C)$ and $M_n(\bb C)$ is nuclear, Proposition \ref{gen implies that $M_n(\cl A)$ satisfies (SP).}}
elements.sent-2411-133: We conclude this section with two results on type $II_1$ factors.
